quality.rationale: 速度空间度规推导+快度参数替换+相对论运动学背景

origin.question:
Problem 6.8. One can put a metric on the velocity space of a particle by defining the distance between two nearby velocities as their relative velocity. Show that the metric can be written in the form
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \mathrm {d} \chi^{2} + \sin h^{2} \chi (\mathrm {d} \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}).
$$
where the magnitude of the velocity is $\mathbf{v} = \tan h \chi$ .
origin.solution:
The relative velocity $\mathrm{ds}$ between two velocities $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{v}} + \mathrm{dy}$ is (by Problem 1.3) given by
$$
d s^{2} = \frac {(d v)^{2} - (v \times d v)^{2}}{(1 - v^{2})^{2}},
$$
and
$$
\left(\underline {{v}} \times d \underline {{v}}\right)^{2} = v^{2} \left(d \underline {{v}}\right)^{2} - \left(\underline {{v}} \cdot d \underline {{v}}\right)^{2}.
$$
Let $	heta$ and $\phi$ be polar and azimuthal angles about the direction of $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ (i.e. $\mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{z}} = \mathbf{v}\cos 	heta, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{v}\sin 	heta\cos \phi, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{v}\sin 	heta\sin \phi$ ). Then
$$
\begin{array}{l}
\left(\mathrm {d} \underline {{v}}\right)^{2} = (\mathrm {d} v)^{2} + v^{2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}\right) \\
\underline {{\mathbf {v}}} \cdot d \underline {\mathbf {v}} = \frac {1}{2} d (\underline {\mathbf {v}} \cdot \underline {\mathbf {v}}) = v d \mathbf {v} \\
\end{array}
$$
so
$$
\mathrm {d s}^{2} = \frac {\mathrm {d v}^{2}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {v}^{2}\right)^{2}} + \frac {\mathrm {v}^{2}}{1 - \mathrm {v}^{2}} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}\right).
$$
Introduce the "rapidity parameter" $\mathbf{v} = \tan h \chi$ . Then
$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {s}^{2} = \mathrm {d} \chi^{2} + \sin h^{2} \chi (\mathrm {d} \theta^{2} + \sin^{2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^{2}).
$$
Note that for small $\mathbf{v}$ , $\sin h \chi \sim \chi \sim \mathbf{v}$ and velocity space is flat, as we expect it to be in the Newtonian limit.
